identity.title: Pipeline Transportation of Refined Petroleum Products

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators that transport refined petroleum products by pipeline for external refiners, marketers, airports, terminals, distributors, or other shippers on a recurring basis.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive refinery or terminal transfer lines, crude-oil pipelines, natural-gas and other-product pipelines, pipeline construction and maintenance contractors, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and businesses outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring tariffed or contracted transportation and related terminal-interface services, generally priced by barrel, distance, product, batch, or committed capacity, with interstate rates commonly subject to FERC ceilings and intrastate service subject to state or contractual regimes.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader NAICS 486 pipeline subsector, not 486910 alone, and does not provide a wage-weighted AI task measure. I infer limited exposure from the substantial field-operator, mechanic, valve, and pumping workforce, then add exposure for scheduling, batch documentation, tariff administration, customer service, finance, regulatory reporting, and integrity-record workflows. Inclusion of gas operations may shift the field mix, so the direction of error is uncertain.
a.rationale: Core throughput depends on physical pumping, control-room monitoring, inspections, maintenance, integrity management, and incident response. BLS identifies large field occupations across pipeline transportation, while cross-industry evidence shows document synthesis, customer care, finance, and knowledge retrieval are plausible AI use cases. These exposed activities are a minority of total wage-weighted work in an asset- and operations-heavy business.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a 486910-specific OEWS staffing matrix and time studies for batch scheduling, nominations, control-room support, tariff billing, quality documentation, integrity records, customer inquiries, and PHMSA/FERC reporting at independent refined-products pipelines.
caveats: BLS data cover NAICS 486 and list common occupations rather than a complete wage-weighted task inventory for 486910.
Corporate-function AI evidence is cross-industry and weighted toward firms much larger than the target lens.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis; it may understate or overstate current labor intensity.

rho.bridge: Cross-industry surveys measure current deployment and expected time to value in corporate and service functions, mostly at large companies, not implementation of the exposed opportunity at 486910 LMM operators. I use low current scaled operations adoption and three-to-five-year value expectations to bound implementation. Larger-company resources likely bias the bridge upward for LMM firms, while vendor-embedded tools may reduce that bias.
rho.rationale: Administrative copilots and workflow automation can be deployed within five years, but operational-technology integration, cybersecurity, product-quality controls, auditability, legacy systems, and safety sign-off constrain implementation. In a 2024 survey only 3% of respondents had scaled an operations-related generative-AI use case, and corporate-function leaders commonly expected three to five years to capture significant value.
rho.change_evidence: Collect pilot-to-production conversion rates, system architecture, cybersecurity approvals, five-year automation budgets, and verified labor-hour savings from refined-products pipeline operators and pipeline-management software vendors.
caveats: Neither adoption source is specific to pipeline transportation or lower-middle-market firms.
Safety reporting obligations demonstrate implementation friction but do not quantify AI deployment.
A small firm may buy mature embedded automation faster than it could develop bespoke AI.

e.rationale: The frozen dataset indicates only four firms in the target EBITDA band. Eligibility is likely reduced by captive refinery or terminal lines, subsidiaries of larger integrated systems, joint ventures, special-purpose entities, and operators without a standalone third-party customer franchise. Some small regional or airport-serving common-carrier systems should still fit the recurring outsourced-service lens.
e.change_evidence: Resolve all four inferred firms against PHMSA operator records, FERC and state tariffs, beneficial ownership, customer lists, related-party throughput, and audited standalone EBITDA.
caveats: No public source directly identifies the four dataset-inferred LMM firms or measures their eligibility.
PHMSA records count reporting systems or operators, not necessarily independent transferable firms.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from size classes and explicitly ESTIMATE.

s5.rationale: A qualifying five-year transfer could arise from succession, infrastructure-fund exits, strategic consolidation, or a refinery/terminal owner divesting logistics assets. However, the eligible universe is probably one or two firms, ownership changes require extensive environmental and safety diligence, and many observed transactions would be parent-level or asset transfers rather than control transfers of a standalone eligible operation.
s5.change_evidence: Build a ten-year transaction census using Capital IQ, PitchBook, Enverus, FERC tariff succession filings, state approvals, PHMSA operator changes, and interviews with the beneficial owners of every eligible target.
caveats: There is no observed control-transfer rate for eligible 486910 LMM firms.
The very small denominator makes realized five-year transfer frequency extremely lumpy.
Internal reorganizations and sales of minority interests or isolated pipe segments do not qualify.

q.bridge: FERC's oil-pipeline index governs annual interstate rate ceilings, not the actual contract mix, realized rates, or customer repricing of each LMM refined-products pipeline. I infer that index-based ceilings decouple rates partly from an individual operator's immediate cost savings, allowing moderate retention; shipper challenges, alternative rate methodologies, contract renewals, and competition can push retention lower.
q.rationale: For the five years beginning July 2026, FERC set the oil-pipeline index at PPI-FG minus 0.55%, and indexed pipelines may charge up to applicable ceilings. This permits some retention of operator-specific efficiency gains because rates do not mechanically reset to each firm's cost, but regulation is explicitly intended to keep rates just and reasonable and competitive shipper negotiations can share benefits.
q.change_evidence: Review each eligible firm's FERC/state tariffs, index eligibility, current ceiling headroom, negotiated and committed-volume contracts, shipper concentration, complaint history, and renewal schedule; model pass-through under the actual revenue mix.
caveats: Intrastate lines and negotiated services may not follow the federal index.
A carrier already pricing below its ceiling may be constrained more by competition than regulation.
This primitive addresses retention after implementation, not whether the operational savings can be achieved.

d5.bridge: EIA forecasts national liquid-fuels consumption and individual fuel trends, while PHMSA reports product-pipeline mileage; neither directly measures constant-quality throughput for the current service basket of LMM 486910 operators. I map roughly flat near-term liquid-fuels demand, declining gasoline, biofuel substitution in distillate, stronger jet-fuel demand, and stable product-pipeline mileage to a slight central decline. Corridor, refinery, airport, and export exposure can move an individual system in either direction.
d5.rationale: EIA's July 2026 STEO projects US liquid-fuels consumption rising only from 20.5 million b/d in 2024 to 20.8 million b/d in 2027, while gasoline consumption is below recent averages. Earlier EIA analysis expected gasoline consumption to fall about 1% in 2026, renewable diesel and biodiesel to reach about 9% of distillate consumption, and jet fuel to reach a record. PHMSA shows refined-products pipeline mileage increasing from 64,231 in 2024 to 65,067 in 2025. The mixed basket supports near-flat demand with downside from efficiency and electrification.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain route-specific shipper commitments, terminal and airport volumes, refinery closure plans, product-level throughput histories, export exposure, EV adoption and fleet-efficiency forecasts, and the line's ability to carry renewable fuels.
caveats: National fuel demand can diverge sharply from a particular pipeline corridor after a refinery closure or terminal reconfiguration.
Liquid-fuels consumption includes products outside the current refined-products service basket.
Mileage measures network stock, not utilization or constant-quality service quantity.

o.rationale: Almost all remaining pipeline throughput still requires an accountable operator for custody transfer, product segregation and quality, pumping and dispatch, integrity management, leak response, tariff administration, and regulatory reporting. AI can reduce labor per barrel but cannot replace the entity responsible for hazardous-liquid infrastructure; only limited demand may shift to self-owned links, other transport modes, or consolidated networks.
o.change_evidence: Interview shippers, pipeline operators, PHMSA personnel, insurers, and terminal managers about outsourcing boundaries, autonomous control, operator-of-record requirements, modal substitution, and planned customer self-provision.
caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share of year-five quantity.
Consolidation may eliminate an independent firm while leaving operator-required service intact.
Fuel-demand erosion and modal substitution belong primarily in d5, not o.

narrative.executive_view: Refined-products pipelines provide recurring, operator-required transport on a stable installed network, but labor is a small share of receipts and the dataset indicates only four LMM-band firms. AI can improve administrative and technical-support workflows; the harder questions are whether any target is truly independent and whether corridor-specific fuel volumes remain durable.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely applications include batch and nomination support, tariff billing, document search, integrity-record review, work-order triage, regulatory drafting, customer inquiries, and finance. Pumping, control-room judgment, inspection, maintenance, product-quality assurance, cybersecurity, and incident response remain human- and operator-accountable.
narrative.value_capture: FERC indexing can let an efficient interstate carrier retain savings because ceilings follow an industry cost index rather than immediate company costs. Actual capture remains sensitive to ceiling headroom, negotiated contracts, shipper bargaining, state regulation, and competition from alternative routes or modes.
narrative.firm_availability: The starting population is only four estimated firms, and captive or integrated systems may leave one or two eligible independent operators. Transfer events are therefore sporadic and each candidate requires entity-level ownership and customer diligence.
narrative.demand_durability: The service remains necessary for gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, and related products, and network mileage is stable to slightly higher. Over five years, declining gasoline and biofuel substitution are offset partly by jet fuel, exports, and overall economic activity; route-level refinery and terminal changes dominate the national average.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Major risks are the tiny target universe, corridor and shipper concentration, refinery closures, electrification and efficiency, product-specification limits for renewable fuels, environmental liabilities, integrity capex, cybersecurity, and rate or contract pass-through. Task exposure and implementation evidence are broader-industry proxies rather than direct measurements.
narrative.principal_driver: Persistent physical distribution of liquid transportation fuels keeps most surviving throughput dependent on an accountable pipeline operator.
narrative.principal_weakness: Only four firms are estimated in band before removing captive and integrated systems, and route-specific demand can fall abruptly after a refinery or terminal change.

sources.title: Pipeline Transportation: NAICS 486
sources.supports: Defines the pipeline subsector and reports 2025 employment in prominent operator, mechanic, pumping, and valve occupations; used as a broader-industry occupation proxy for a.

sources.title: Gen AI in corporate functions: Looking beyond efficiency gains
sources.supports: Reports a 2024 survey of 276 corporate-function leaders, AI use cases, partial-role automation, barriers, and a three-to-five-year value horizon; supports a and rho by proxy.

sources.title: From promising to productive: Real results from gen AI in services
sources.supports: Reports that only 3% of surveyed large-company respondents had scaled an operations-related gen-AI use case in early 2024 and discusses governance and change constraints; supports rho by proxy.

sources.title: Annual Report Mileage for Hazardous Liquid or Carbon Dioxide Systems
sources.supports: Reports 65,067 miles of petroleum/refined-products systems in 2025 versus 64,231 in 2024 and 656 hazardous-liquid records in 2025; supports network durability and operator diligence.

sources.title: FERC Finalizes Five-Year Review of the Oil Pipeline Index
sources.supports: States that the 2026-2031 oil-pipeline index is PPI-FG minus 0.55%, that indexed carriers may charge up to applicable ceilings, and that the regime aims to reflect industry costs and keep rates just and reasonable; supports q.

sources.title: Short-Term Energy Outlook: U.S. Petroleum Products, July 2026
sources.supports: Projects US liquid-fuels consumption of 20.5 million b/d in 2024 and 20.8 million b/d in 2027 and describes below-average gasoline consumption; supports d5 by proxy.

sources.title: Refinery closures and rising consumption will reduce U.S. petroleum inventories in 2026
sources.supports: Forecasts a roughly 1% gasoline-consumption decline in 2026, biofuels at about 9% of distillate consumption, and record jet-fuel consumption; supports the mixed refined-products demand outlook.
